# Ryno Tools — Full Content Guide

Last updated: 2026-07-02

Ryno Tools is a free, offline-capable Progressive Web App for certification exam preparation. No login required, no paywalls, no tracking.

## Overview

The platform provides adaptive microlearning through flashcard quizzes and timed exam simulations. Each category has a dedicated hub page with deep content, FAQs, and direct links to the quiz.

## Quizzes Available

<!-- quizzes:start -->
### Ham Radio

Hub: https://ryno.tools/ham-radio/

  - **Technician** (409+ questions): Entry-level FCC license covering regulations, operating procedures, and electronics fundamentals. Uses the current 2026-2030 NCVEC pool (409 questions, effective July 1, 2026). Required for VHF/UHF band access. Typical study time: 10–20 hours.
  - **General** (456+ questions): Intermediate license that unlocks HF band privileges. Covers propagation, antenna theory, advanced operating procedures, and FCC regulations. Typical study time: 20–30 hours.
  - **Extra** (706+ questions): Top-tier FCC license covering advanced regulations, EMI, transmission theory, and expanded privileges. Typical study time: 40–60 hours.

### Falconry

Hub: https://ryno.tools/falconry/

  - **Apprentice** (398+ questions): Preparation for the falconry apprentice exam required in all 50 U.S. states. Covers raptor biology, mews housing, health and nutrition, training, and federal/state hunting regulations (50 CFR Part 21).

### Security+

Hub: https://ryno.tools/security-plus/

  - **Security+ SY0-701** (100+ questions): 100 practice questions aligned to CompTIA Security+ SY0-701 objectives: threats and vulnerabilities, cryptography, access control, network security, incident response, and governance and compliance.

### DevOps

Hub: https://ryno.tools/devops/

  - **Core Concepts & Culture** (50+ questions): 50 questions covering DevOps culture, CI/CD principles, SRE and reliability engineering (error budgets, SLI/SLO/SLA), Agile and Lean fundamentals, and DORA metrics — sourced from Google SRE Book, Accelerate, Team Topologies, and the Agile Manifesto.
  - **Containers & Kubernetes** (53+ questions): 53 questions covering container fundamentals, image layers and registries, Kubernetes pods and workloads, services and networking, storage (PV/PVC), and troubleshooting (CrashLoopBackOff, OOMKilled, ImagePullBackOff) — sourced from kubernetes.io and docs.docker.com.
  - **Linux Fundamentals** (50+ questions): 50 questions covering process management, filesystems and storage, users and permissions, networking basics, and shell and text processing — sourced from Linux man pages, kernel.org, Red Hat docs, and the Filesystem Hierarchy Standard.
  - **Networking for DevOps** (50+ questions): 50 questions covering DNS and name resolution, TCP/IP and routing, TLS and certificate management, load balancers and proxies, and HTTP and application protocols — sourced from RFCs, NGINX docs, Cloudflare Learning Center, and MDN.
  - **CI/CD Pipelines** (50+ questions): 50 questions covering pipeline fundamentals, GitHub Actions, Jenkins and GitLab CI, testing and quality gates, and deployment strategies (blue/green, canary, rolling, feature flags) — sourced from GitHub Actions docs, GitLab CI docs, Jenkins docs, and Humble & Farley's Continuous Delivery.
  - **Infrastructure as Code** (50+ questions): 50 questions covering Terraform core concepts and state management, Ansible fundamentals and advanced patterns, and IaC principles including Pulumi, GitOps, immutable infrastructure, and policy-as-code — sourced from Terraform docs, Ansible docs, Pulumi docs, and Kief Morris's Infrastructure as Code.
  - **Cloud Platforms** (50+ questions): 50 questions covering cloud fundamentals, AWS core services (EC2, S3, IAM, RDS/DynamoDB), GCP and Azure patterns, networking and security (VPC, WAF, KMS), and architecture patterns (HA, DR, RPO/RTO, serverless) — sourced from AWS, GCP, and Azure Well-Architected Frameworks.
  - **Monitoring & Observability** (50+ questions): 50 questions covering metrics and dashboards (Prometheus, Grafana), logging (structured logs, ELK, Loki), distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry, Jaeger), alerting and on-call practices, and reliability concepts (SLI/SLO/SLA, error budgets, four golden signals) — sourced from OpenTelemetry spec, Google SRE Book, and Prometheus docs.
<!-- quizzes:end -->

## Japanese Learning

Hub: https://ryno.tools/japanese/

Structured interactive curriculum following Japan's actual 6-3-3 school system (6 elementary + 3 junior high + 3 high school = 13 path nodes). Content is anchored to MEXT (Japan's Ministry of Education) kokugo standards and the JLPT N5→N1 proficiency ladder.

### Currently Available

**Foundations**
- Hiragana (104 characters): Full gojūon grid — 46 base characters + 58 with voiced marks (dakuten: が、ざ、だ、ば) and combination characters (きゃ、しゅ、ちょ). Each character has a vivid mnemonic image.
- Katakana (104 characters): The angular script used for loanwords — コーヒー (coffee), テレビ (TV), アイスクリーム (ice cream). Same structure as hiragana; mnemonics emphasize angular shape differences.

**Elementary 1 (小学1年)**
- Kanji Grade 1 (80 characters): The 80 kyōiku kanji taught in Japanese first grade — numbers (一、二、三…), nature (山、川、木), people and relationships (人、父、母), and daily life (日、月、年).
- Vocabulary I (24 words): Greetings (こんにちは, ありがとう, すみません), colors (あか, あお, きいろ, しろ, くろ, みどり), and core everyday words.
- Numbers & Counters (50 items): 1–10,000 including sound-change rules (さんびゃく/ろっぴゃく/はっぴゃく, さんぜん); 10 essential counters (〜つ native counting, 〜人 with irregulars ひとり/ふたり, 〜本/枚/匹/個/台/冊); time (〜時/〜分/半, 午前/午後); days of the week + きょう/あした/きのう.
- Sentences I (40 N5 grammar patterns, 4 units):
  - Statements: AはBです, これは〜です, 〜ではありません, も, い/な-adjectives
  - Questions: 〜ですか, question words (なん/どこ/だれ/いつ), はい/いいえ answers, なんじ (what time), いくら (how much)
  - Doing things: を/に/へ/で particles with first ます-form verbs (たべます, いきます, みます, かえります, べんきょうします)
  - This & That: これ/それ/あれ/どれ, この/その/あの/どの, あります/います, 〜てください, なにも〜ない

### Coming Soon

- Elementary 2–6: Kanji grades 2–6 (160, 200, 202, 193, 191 characters per grade; 1,026 total). Grammar: て-form verbs (Gr2), adjective conjugation and giving/receiving あげる/もらう (Gr3), potential form/〜たい/〜たら (Gr4), keigo introduction/いらっしゃる (Gr5), passive voice preview (Gr6).
- Junior High 1–3: Remaining ~1,110 jōyō kanji (~370 per grade, reaching 2,136 total). Grammar: full passive and causative forms (JH1), complete keigo system — sonkeigo/kenjōgo/teineigo (JH2), classical grammar introduction and N3-level reading (JH3).
- High School 1–3: N2 grammar patterns and register mastery (HS1), classical Japanese 古文 from Man'yōshū/Genji Monogatari (HS2), Sino-Japanese 漢文 kanbun + N1 grammar (HS3). No new kanji — jōyō set is complete at the end of junior high.

### How It Works

Three active-recall practice modes per skill:
- **Learn**: Flashcard with reading, meaning, mnemonic, and example sentence. Self-rated (got it / still learning).
- **Match**: Multiple-choice — see the Japanese prompt, pick the correct meaning from 4 options. Distractors drawn from the same lesson for a stiffer challenge.
- **Type**: Romaji input — see the full sentence or word with a blank (◯), type the answer in romaji. Grammar items show the cloze sentence; irregular particle pronunciations (は→wa, を→o, へ→e) accepted as correct.

Progress tracked per skill in browser localStorage. Mastery threshold: 3 correct answers per item. No account required, no server calls.

## How the App Works

### Study Mode

Users select a quiz category and subcategory (e.g., "Ham Radio" → "Technician"). Questions appear one at a time. Users can:
- Select an answer and get instant feedback
- Filter questions by tag (e.g., show only "safety" questions)
- Review explanations after answering
- Track personal progress (questions answered, percentage correct)
- Toggle between light and dark mode
- Pause and resume at any time

State is saved in browser session storage; progress persists across browser sessions.

### Exam Mode

Timed exam simulation with random question selection and scoring:
- Time limit and question count set per exam specification
- No pausing or reviewing during the exam
- Pass/fail result shown with score breakdown by topic
- Detailed performance report (questions answered, time per question)

### Question Schema

Each question includes:
```
{
  "question": "question text",
  "choices": ["choice A", "choice B", "choice C", "choice D"],
  "correct": "choice A",
  "explanation": "detailed explanation",
  "category": "ham-radio",
  "subcategory": "technician",
  "topic_label": "Operating Procedures",
  "topic_id": "OP.2",
  "type": "multiple",
  "tags": ["safety", "operating-procedures"],
  "image": "" // optional image URL
}
```

## Technology

**Client-side only**: Ryno Tools is a static Progressive Web App (PWA) built with vanilla ES6 JavaScript. No backend server, no database, no API calls (except optional crypto price widget).

**Offline capable**: The service worker caches the app shell and all datasets. Users can study without internet after the first visit.

**Installation**: Users can install as a native-like app on mobile and desktop via their browser's PWA menu.

**Storage**: Session state stored in browser sessionStorage; theme preference in localStorage.

**Privacy**: No user accounts, no analytics beyond what's set in Google Analytics, no cookies for tracking. All study happens locally in the browser.
